A patient lies on a
table in a hospital
treatment room. The
door is closed.
The room is empty
except for the patient
and the machine. In the
next room, an operator
sits at a console.
She has entered the
treatment parameters.
The screen confirms
the settings. She
presses the key to
activate the beam.
The machine fires. The
screen updates:
treatment complete.
Normal dose.
No error. The patient
feels a sudden,
intense burning like a
hot wire pressed
against the skin.
She calls out. The
intercom is off.
She gets up. She finds
someone.
She says something
went wrong.
The operator checks
the console.
The readout shows a
routine treatment. The
dose log is normal. The
machine recorded no
fault.
One of them is wrong.
The patient, or the
machine.
The hospital believes
the machine.
This is a story about
what happens when a
system earns trust it
was never tested
enough to
deserve and what it
costs when that trust
finally breaks.
Radiation therapy
works by aiming a
beam of high-energy
particles
at a tumor. The goal is
to deliver enough
energy to destroy
cancerous cells while
leaving the surrounding
tissue intact.
The margin is narrow.
Too little dose and the
treatment fails.
Too much and you burn
healthy tissue,
damage organs, kill the
patient.
The machine exists to
hold that margin.
A medical linear
accelerator can operate
in two modes.
In electron mode, the
beam hits the patient
directly useful
for tumors near the
surface.
In X-ray mode, the
electron beam strikes a
metal target
first, which converts it
into X-rays that can
penetrate deeper.
In X-ray mode, a
flattening filter
spreads the beam
evenly
across the treatment
area. The distinction
matters.
In electron mode,
the beam is relatively
low energy and diffuse.
In X-ray mode without
the target and filter in
place,
the full-power electron
beam would hit the
patient concentrated
and
unshielded. That is not
a treatment.
That is an injury. The
machine's job is to
make sure the beam
mode
and the physical
hardware configuration
always match.
If the operator selects
X-ray, the target and
filter must be in the
beam path.
If they are not, the
machine must not fire.
That rule is the entire
safety architecture.
Everything depends on
it being enforced.
The Therac-25 was not
a new machine. It was
the third in a series
built by Atomic Energy
of Canada Limited.
Its predecessors the
Therac-6 and Therac-20
had
been in clinical
use for years. Those
earlier machines had
software controlling
the beam. But they also
had something else:
hardware interlocks.
Physical mechanisms
in the beam path that
prevented dangerous
configurations from
reaching the
patient,
regardless of what the
software did.
If the software set up
the wrong mode, the
hardware caught it. A
mechanical fuse
between intention and
consequence.
The software could
fail, and the patient
would still be safe.
The Therac-25 removed
the hardware
interlocks.
Software would handle
safety directly.
No physical backup. No
mechanical check. The
reasoning was
straightforward: the
software
had been used in
the earlier models. It
had worked. It had
never caused an
accident. Why duplicate
safety with expensive
hardware
when the code was
already proven?
That reasoning had a
flaw. The software in
the earlier machines
had never been tested
operating alone.
It had always run
behind the hardware
safety net.
The fact that no
software failure had
caused an accident did
not mean the software
could not
fail. It meant the
hardware had never
needed to catch
a failure visibly.
The absence of a
visible problem was
treated as proof that
no problem existed.
The safety net was
removed because it had
never been
seen catching anything.
And the machine
shipped.
The Therac-25's
software had a flaw
that only appeared
under
specific conditions. The
operator interface let
you type in treatment
parameters, then edit
them. If you typed a
command, moved the
cursor up to change a
field,
and re-entered the
correct value within a
narrow time window
roughly eight seconds
the software could
enter a state where
the beam mode
displayed on screen no
longer matched the
physical hardware
configuration. The
screen would show
X-ray mode.
The machine would be
configured for direct
electron beam.
No target in the path.
No flattening filter.
Full-power electrons
aimed straight at the
patient.
This was a race
condition.
A timing-dependent bug.
The software used a
shared variable that
could be overwritten if
the operator edited
faster than the
machine could
reconfigure.
The counter that
tracked the setup
process would
overflow, skip the
safety check,
and leave the hardware
in the wrong state.
In the earlier Therac
models, this would not
have mattered.
The hardware interlock
would have physically
blocked the beam.
In the Therac-25, there
was nothing between
the bug and
the patient. The
overdose was massive.
Patients received
doses estimated at 100
times the intended
amount,
concentrated into a
beam a few millimeters
wide.
In a fraction of a
second. And the
console would display:
treatment complete.
No error.
Sometimes a cryptic
message appeared
MALFUNCTION
54 with no explanation
of what it meant, no
indication of severity,
and a prompt that
allowed the operator to
simply press
P to proceed.
Operators learned to
press P.
The message came up
often enough to feel
routine.
It was background
noise. Here is how a
machine earns trust it
does not deserve.
Not through deception.
Through repetition.
Layer one: inherited
reputation. The
Therac-25's software
was carried forward
from earlier models.
Code that has been
running in the field
feels tested.
It carries a track
record. But the track
record belonged to a
different context one
where hardware
interlocks caught any
software failure before
it reached
the patient. The
reputation was real.
The safety it implied
was not.
Layer two: removed
safeguards.
The hardware
interlocks were taken
out. This was framed
as simplification.
An improvement.
Fewer moving parts.
Lower cost. No one
performed a
systematic analysis of
what
the software would
need to handle on its
own, because no one
believed the software
would fail
in a way that mattered.
Layer three: successful
operation. The machine
shipped to hospitals.
It ran treatments.
Hundreds of them.
Thousands. Treatments
completed. Patients
received their doses.
No alarms. Each
successful session
reinforced the same
conclusion: the machine
works. Layer four:
normalized error
messages.
When errors did appear,
they were cryptic.
MALFUNCTION 54.
No explanation.
No severity level.
No instruction to stop
treatment and
investigate.
Operators saw these
messages regularly.
They learned that
pressing P and retrying
usually worked. The
interface trained them
to override warnings,
because the warnings
had never meant
anything
before.
Layer five: the
instrument over the
patient.
When patients reported
pain burning,
stinging, sensations
that did not match a
normal treatment the
machine's dose log
showed a normal
delivery.
The data said
everything was fine.
The patient said it was
not. And in a system
built on the assumption
that the
machine was reliable,
the data won.
This chain inherited
trust, removed
safeguards,
reinforced by
repetition, normalized
errors,
instrument over patient
is the same structural
pattern as Challenger's
warning-softening
chain, running in a
completely different
domain.
In both cases, the
system was not lying.
It was not hiding
anything. It was simply
operating on a
definition of
safety that had never
been stress-tested
against the
conditions that would
break it. The conditions
arrived.
Between June 1985 and
January 1987,
the Therac-25 was
involved in at least six
known
radiation
overdose incidents at
hospitals in the United
States and Canada.
Three patients died
from their injuries.
Others suffered severe
radiation burns,
neurological damage,
and long-term
disability.
The first reported
accident was at a clinic
in Georgia
in 1985. A patient
received a massive
overdose. She
developed radiation
burns consistent with
far more energy than
the prescribed
treatment. The hospital
reported the incident to
the
manufacturer, AECL.
AECL investigated.
They could not
reproduce the error.
Their conclusion: the
machine's safety
systems made such an
overdose impossible.
The problem must have
been elsewhere. A
second incident
occurred at a different
hospital.
Same symptoms. Same
machine behavior.
Treatment complete.
No error.
AECL again could not
reproduce the fault.
Again concluded the
machine was not
responsible.
Then came the incident
that cracked the case
open.
An operator in Tyler,
Texas, had noticed
something. When she
entered parameters in
a specific
sequence typing one
mode, then quickly
correcting to another
the machine displayed
MALFUNCTION 54 and
delivered
only a partial dose.
She would press P,
retry, and the same
thing happened. On one
occasion she retried
three times.
The patient, lying on
the table, received
three massive
overdoses in
succession.
He died five months
later. The operator
reported the exact
keystroke
sequence.
She had identified the
trigger. AECL issued a
software patch.
The patch addressed
that specific input
sequence.
More accidents
followed. The patch had
fixed one path to
the bug, but the
underlying architecture
the race condition,
the shared variable, the
missing hardware
check was still there.
Other keystroke
sequences could trigger
the same failure.
Each incident was
handled as a one-off.
Each investigation
started from the
assumption that the
machine was
fundamentally sound.
And no mechanism
existed to connect
reports across
hospitals. A physicist
at one clinic had no
way of
knowing that a
physicist at another
clinic, a thousand miles
away, was seeing the
same
thing.
The pattern was there.
No one was looking at
the whole picture.
The investigation that
finally exposed the full
scope of the
problem was driven
largely by a hospital
physicist named Fritz
Hager at the East
Texas Cancer Center.
After the Tyler
incidents, Hager
refused to accept
AECL's assurance
that the machine was
safe. He conducted his
own tests.
He replicated the
operator's keystroke
sequence. He measured
the output. He found
that the machine could
deliver
radiation doses orders
of magnitude above
what the console
displayed.
He contacted the FDA.
He contacted other
hospitals. He started
connecting incidents
that had been
treated as unrelated.
What Hager
demonstrated was not
just a technical
defect. It was a
structural failure: the
manufacturer's
investigation process
could
not find the problem
because it started from
the assumption that
the machine was
reliable. Every test
was designed to
confirm safety,
not to probe for failure.
The question was never
"under what conditions
could this machine
hurt someone?" The
question was "can we
reproduce the specific
reported error?" And
when they could not,
the case was closed.
The same inversion of
proof that appeared in
the
Challenger decision
meeting. The burden
was on the failure to
prove itself, not on the
system to prove its
safety.
Once the full picture
emerged, the failures
were not subtle.
The software had no
independent safety
monitoring.
The same code that
controlled the beam
also decided whether
the beam was safe.
There was no separate
system watching for
dangerous states. The
error messages shown
to operators were
meaningless.
Numeric codes with no
description,
no severity ranking, no
instruction to stop.
The interface was not
designed to
communicate danger.
It was designed to
communicate workflow
status.
There was no hardware
dose monitor that could
independently detect an
overdose in real time.
The machine trusted
its own software to
report
its own errors. That is
like asking someone to
grade their own
exam. The
manufacturer had not
performed a
systematic
failure-mode analysis
on the software.
No one had asked: what
happens if the setup
routine
is interrupted? What
happens if the operator
edits faster than
the machine
reconfigures? What
happens if the counter
overflows?
And the FDA's
regulatory framework
was not equipped to
evaluate
software-dependent
medical devices. The
agency had authority
over the
hardware. The
software lived in a gap.
Two causes. A
software defect that
allowed a lethal
mismatch between
beam mode and
hardware configuration.
And an organizational
system manufacturer,
regulator, hospital that
was structured to
confirm the machine
worked
rather than to discover
how it could fail.
The Therac-25 did not
fail despite the system.
It failed because the
system was designed
to trust itself.
The Therac-25 is now
one of the most cited
case
studies in software
engineering, medical
device regulation,
and safety system
design. Not because it
was the worst
accident.
Because the pattern it
revealed keeps showing
up.
Carried-forward code
inherits a reputation it
may not have earned in
the new context.
Removing a safety
layer is treated as
simplification,
not as a change that
requires its own risk
analysis.
Operators adapt to
unreliable signals by
learning to ignore them
which means real
warnings get the same
response as false
alarms. And when a
machine says one thing
and a
person says another,
the default is to believe
the machine.
The case did produce
real change.
The FDA expanded its
authority to cover
software in medical
devices. The principle
of defense in depth
never rely on a single
layer for safety was
reinforced as a
binding design standard
for radiation equipment.
Independent hardware
dose monitors became
expected,
not optional. And
incident-reporting
mechanisms across
hospitals were
strengthened so that
patterns invisible to
individual sites could
be
caught. But the deeper
question the one that
makes this more than a
historical case is how
we build
trust in systems that
are too complex to fully
test.
Autonomous vehicles
run millions of miles
without incident,
and each mile builds
confidence. But the
confidence is in the
conditions that have
been
driven, not in the
conditions that have
not.
Algorithmic decision
systems in healthcare
and criminal justice
compile
track records of
outputs, and those
track records become
the
evidence of reliability.
But the track record
only covers what has
been measured. The
question is always the
same.
Has the system been
tested under the
conditions where it
could fail?
Or only under the
conditions where it has
succeeded?
The Therac-25
answered that question
with six patients who
did not know they were
the test. The most
dangerous kind of trust
is the kind that has
never been tested.
Only repeated.
The Therac-25 did not
fail because the people
around it were
careless. It failed
because every layer of
the system the
engineers who carried
the code forward,
the manufacturer who
removed the interlocks,
the operators who
learned to override the
errors, the regulators
who had not caught up
to software had
built confidence on the
same foundation: the
machine had worked
before. That was true.
It was also incomplete.
And incomplete, in a
system where the
consequences are
measured in human
tissue, is not the same
as safe.
The failure was real.
The cause chain was
longer.
Next time: another
design failure.
Another system that
taught itself to ignore
what it already
knew.

which means real
warnings get the same
response as false
alarms. And when a
machine says one thing
and a
person says another,
the default is to believe
the machine.
The case did produce
real change.
The FDA expanded its
authority to cover
software in medical
devices. The principle
of defense in depth
never rely on a single
layer for safety was
reinforced as a
binding design standard
for radiation equipment.
Independent hardware
dose monitors became
expected,
not optional. And
incident-reporting
mechanisms across
hospitals were
strengthened so that
patterns invisible to
individual sites could
be
caught. But the deeper
question the one that
makes this more than a
historical case is how
we build
trust in systems that
are too complex to fully
test.
Autonomous vehicles
run millions of miles
without incident,
and each mile builds
confidence. But the
confidence is in the
conditions that have
been
driven, not in the
conditions that have
not.
Algorithmic decision
systems in healthcare
and criminal justice
compile
track records of
outputs, and those
track records become
the
evidence of reliability.
But the track record
only covers what has
been measured. The
question is always the
same.
Has the system been
tested under the
conditions where it
could fail?
Or only under the
conditions where it has
succeeded?
The Therac-25
answered that question
with six patients who
did not know they were
the test. The most
dangerous kind of trust
is the kind that has
never been tested.
Only repeated.
The Therac-25 did not
fail because the people
around it were
careless. It failed
because every layer of
the system the
engineers who carried
the code forward,
the manufacturer who
removed the interlocks,
the operators who
learned to override the
errors, the regulators
who had not caught up
to software had
built confidence on the
same foundation: the
machine had worked
before. That was true.
It was also incomplete.
And incomplete, in a
system where the
consequences are
measured in human
tissue, is not the same
as safe.
The failure was real.
The cause chain was
longer.
Next time: another
design failure.
Another system that
taught itself to ignore
what it already
knew.
